text:
REFINED BRIEFING (events grouped by probative direction).
Retrieval hints: actors=['Donald Trump', 'U.S. Supreme Court', 'D.C. Circuit Court of Appeals', 'Department of Justice', 'Department of Homeland Security'], topics=['immigration', 'deportation', 'alien enemies act', 'judicial review', 'executive power', 'supreme court'].

## BACKGROUND (12)
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, his first day in office, President Donald Trump signed multiple executive actions. He pardoned approximately 1,500 individuals convicted in the Jan. 6, 2021 Capitol attack, commuted sentences for 14 others, and ordered an end to federal cases against his political opponents. Trump reinstated the "Remain in Mexico" policy, ended the CBP One app, and declared a national emergency at the southern border, also designating certain cartels as foreign terrorists. He issued an order aiming to end birthright citizenship and suspended the U.S. Refugee Admissions Program. In energy policy, Trump withdrew the U.S. from the Paris Climate Agreement, revoked several climate-related executive orders, declared a national energy emergency, and lifted the moratorium on new liquefied natural gas export licenses. He also temporarily withdrew areas on the Outer Continental Shelf from offshore wind leasing and began withdrawing the U.S. from the World Health Organization. Other actions included rescinding 78 Biden administration policies, implementing a federal hiring freeze, and issuing a proclamation recognizing only two sexes. These actions prompted immediate legal challenges.
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump signed multiple executive orders on his first day in office. These actions included declaring a national emergency at the Mexico–United States border, blocking asylum seekers, and ending the "catch and release" policy for undocumented individuals. Executive Order 14165 reinstated the Migrant Protection Protocols ("Remain in Mexico"), terminated the CBP One mobile application, and ended categorical parole programs. An executive order directed the end of birthright citizenship for children born on or after February 19, 2025, to parents without lawful permanent residence or U.S. citizenship. The measure is facing a lawsuit. Refugee admissions were suspended, effective January 27, 2025, and international cartels were designated as terrorists. Executive Order 14159, titled 'Protecting the American People Against Invasion,' revoked previous Biden administration immigration proclamations and mandated actions on interior enforcement and detention. Separately, an executive order reinstated and amended Executive Order 13957, renaming "Schedule F" to "Schedule Policy/Career" within the federal workforce. This order stated that employees must faithfully implement administration policies, with failure constituting grounds for dismissal, and revoked Executive Order 14003. The Director of the Office of Personnel Management was directed to amend Civil Service Regulations. The President also initiated a review of the USMCA trade agreement and other trade agreements.
- [2025-01-20] President Trump, following his inauguration on January 20, 2025, issued a series of executive actions. He directed the Secretary of State to align U.S. foreign policy with an "America First" directive, prioritizing American interests and citizens. On immigration, actions included "realigning" the Refugee Admission Program, designating cartels as foreign terrorist organizations, declaring a national emergency at the southern border, and directing construction of a border wall. The administration also moved to end "catch and release," reinstate "Remain in Mexico," and suspend refugee resettlement from January 27.

Regarding federal employees, Trump mandated all executive branch personnel return to in-person, full-time work, ending remote arrangements. He also imposed a 90-day hiring freeze on federal civilian employees, effective January 20, 2025, for unfilled positions, with exemptions for national security and immigration enforcement. The freeze excludes military personnel and prohibits using third-party contractors to bypass it. Additionally, Trump initiated actions to withdraw the U.S. from the Paris Agreement, declared a national energy emergency, and temporarily halted new wind energy projects on federal lands and waters. These orders also paused funding from the Inflation Reduction Act and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump issued executive orders, memoranda, and proclamations. Actions included a presidential proclamation declaring an "invasion" at the southern border, suspending migrant entry and asylum requests. Executive Order 14165 "Securing Our Borders" reinstated the "Remain in Mexico" program, restarted border wall construction, and limited humanitarian parole. A national emergency was declared at the border, directing military deployment. An executive order also sought to reinterpret the 14th Amendment regarding birthright citizenship, facing immediate legal challenges.

In energy and environmental policy, Executive Order 14154 "Unleashing American Energy" promoted fossil fuel production and critical mineral development, and ended federal support for electric vehicles. The U.S. withdrew from the Paris Agreement and the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change. A national energy emergency was declared to expedite fossil fuel projects.

Regarding federal operations, a hiring freeze was implemented across most executive branch agencies. A "Regulatory Freeze Pending Review" was enacted for new rules. Federal employees were directed to return to in-person work. An executive order defined sex as an immutable biological classification, directing agencies to remove materials promoting "gender ideology." The administration also established an "America First Trade Policy" and revoked 58 executive orders and 10 presidential memoranda from the previous administration, targeting diversity, equity, and inclusion policies.
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump signed a series of executive orders, memorandums, and proclamations. These actions included pardoning approximately 1,500 individuals involved in the January 6 Capitol siege and commuting sentences for 14 others, directing the Justice Department to dismiss related pending cases. Trump also paused the TikTok divest-or-ban law for 75 days and rescinded 78 executive actions from the previous administration covering racial equality, climate, and migration.

New orders directed agencies to seek price relief to address inflation. Immigration policy changes included declaring a national emergency at the southern border via Proclamation 10886, deploying armed forces to assist the Department of Homeland Security, ending "catch-and-release," reinstating "Remain in Mexico," designating cartels as terrorist organizations, and suspending refugee resettlement.

The President also declared a national energy emergency, directing agencies to expedite domestic energy resource development, including crude oil, natural gas, and critical minerals, and infrastructure projects. Other actions reinstated the federal death penalty, froze federal hiring, mandated federal workers return to office, initiated withdrawal from the Paris Climate Accord, defined sex as biologically male or female for government policy, and ended federal diversity, equity, and inclusion programs.
- [2025-01-20] President Donald Trump signed multiple executive orders on January 20, 2025, initiating policy changes across energy, immigration, and federal oversight.

In energy policy, the orders directed changes to expedite environmental reviews and permitting for fossil fuels, nuclear, and geothermal technologies. The orders also paused federal spending and incentives for clean energy programs authorized under the Inflation Reduction Act and Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. An Office of Management and Budget memorandum formalized this pause on January 27, leading to a reported stop in Medicaid funding to states on January 28.

Regarding immigration, Trump signed 10 executive orders, many reviving policies from his first administration. These actions included pausing refugee resettlement, ending humanitarian parole for over 500,000 immigrants, and launching efforts to remove unauthorized immigrants, resulting in approximately 8,200 arrests between January 20 and February 2. Policies set in motion included restarting the Migration Protection Protocols and expanding expedited removal.

A separate executive order, titled 'Ending the Weaponization of the Federal Government,' directed the Attorney General and the Director of National Intelligence to review federal law enforcement and intelligence community activities over the past four years, identifying conduct contrary to the order's stated purpose and recommending remedial actions. Multiple lawsuits have challenged these executive actions.
- [2025-01-20] President Donald Trump signed multiple executive orders and memoranda on January 20, 2025, his first day of a second term, addressing various policy areas.

Actions included revoking President Bill Clinton's Executive Order 12898 on environmental justice and rescinding several Biden administration executive orders related to climate change, racial equity, and the Justice40 Initiative. Trump declared a "national energy emergency," promoted domestic energy production, temporarily halted offshore wind energy leasing, and initiated the process to withdraw the U.S. from the Paris Agreement. Orders also directed agencies to lower energy prices and paused certain funds from the Inflation Reduction Act and Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act.

An executive order titled "Ending Radical and Wasteful Government DEI Programs and Preferencing" directed federal agencies to terminate diversity, equity, inclusion, and accessibility programs. Other directives included "Expanding Educational Freedom and Opportunity for Families," focusing on school choice, and a federal hiring freeze.

An executive order titled "PROTECTING THE AMERICAN PEOPLE AGAINST INVASION" revoked four prior executive orders from early 2021, directing agencies to prioritize immigration law enforcement against inadmissible and removable aliens, and establishing Homeland Security Task Forces. The administration also rescinded 78 Biden-era executive actions, initiated withdrawal from the World Health Organization, reaffirmed the Hyde Amendment, and issued the "America First Trade Policy" memorandum.
- [2025-01-20] Donald Trump began his second term as President of the United States on January 20, 2025, signing 26 executive orders. On that day, he declared a "national energy emergency" and initiated the United States' withdrawal from the 2015 Paris Climate Agreement. He signed Executive Order 14154, "Unleashing American Energy," directing federal agencies to revise regulations on domestic energy resources and revoking the American Climate Corps.

Other initial actions included initiating withdrawal from the World Health Organization, revising federal recognition of gender identity, establishing the Department of Government Efficiency, pardoning over 1,500 individuals involved in the January 6 Capitol riot, designating Mexican drug cartels as foreign terrorist organizations, and declaring a national emergency at the southern border.

The administration also announced cabinet and cabinet-level nominations, including Marco Rubio for Secretary of State and Pamela Bondi for Attorney General. Individuals with backgrounds as oil, gas, and chemical lobbyists were appointed to the Environmental Protection Agency. Some of these orders have been blocked in court.
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump signed executive orders and memoranda, his first actions upon beginning his second term. These directives rescinded numerous policies from the previous administration, including 78 executive orders and presidential memorandums issued between January 21, 2021, and January 19, 2025.

One order, titled "Initial Rescissions of Harmful Executive Orders and Actions," revoked measures related to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI), immigration, climate, and federal workforce management. Specific rescinded orders included those on ethics pledges (EO 13989), census data tabulation (EO 13986), racial equity (EO 13985), gender identity/sexual orientation discrimination (EO 13988), and worker health and safety (EO 13999). Agency heads were directed to cease DEI program implementation.

Another order, titled "Defending Women from Gender Ideology Extremism and Restoring Biological Truth to the Federal Government," established a federal policy recognizing two sexes, male and female, defined biologically at conception, and mandated agencies rescind contrary guidance. Further directives included declaring a national emergency at the southern border, terminating Green New Deal initiatives, and implementing a federal hiring freeze. The Domestic Policy Council and National Economic Council directors were tasked with reviewing actions under revoked orders for further rescissions within 45 days.
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, President Trump signed multiple executive actions. Executive Order 14165, titled "Securing Our Borders," mandated physical barriers, increased border personnel, expanded detention facilities, and reinstated "Remain in Mexico" policies. The order also called for ceasing CBP One use, terminating parole programs, and increasing prosecutions for border-related crimes.

Separately, Trump issued executive orders on energy policy. He declared a national energy emergency, citing high prices, which enabled powers like the Defense Production Act. Other orders reduced regulatory burdens, lifted the moratorium on new liquefied natural gas export licenses, and addressed Alaska's natural resource utilization. The administration also directed the U.S. withdrawal from the Paris Climate Agreement and temporarily withdrew Outer Continental Shelf areas from offshore wind leasing.

A Presidential Memorandum initiated a hiring freeze for federal civilian employees across the executive branch, prohibiting the filling of vacant or new positions. Exemptions included military, immigration enforcement, national security, and public safety roles. The freeze was initially set to expire for most agencies within 90 days, pending an Office of Management and Budget (OMB) workforce reduction plan, but was later extended.

He pardoned approximately 1,500 individuals convicted in the Jan. 6, 2021 Capitol attack, commuted sentences for 14 others, and ordered an end to federal cases against his political opponents.
